# SSH and Remote Compute

Chapter 1 promised a motion: the analysis leaves the laptop,
runs on a remote Linux machine where the heavy work happens,
and the results come back. Chapter 13 ended on the other half
of that promise, that `uv sync` and `renv::restore()` would
rebuild the project's environment "when this project moves to a
remote server." This chapter is where both promises come due.
The tool that makes it possible is `ssh`: the one interface
that makes a machine you cannot see behave like Chapter 2's
local shell.

The spine of the chapter is a single idea: the same project
and the same workflow, on a machine you cannot see, with the
Linux boundary flagged where it counts. `ssh` opens a shell on
the remote box and runs commands there; a key and a config
file make the connection authenticated and repeatable; `rsync`
moves the project across and the results back without
destroying either side; the Chapter 13 lockfiles rebuild the
environment on arrival; and you learn to recognize the two
larger remote worlds, GPU pods and HPC schedulers, without this
book pretending to teach them in depth. Everything here is
client-side. You are a guest on someone else's Linux box,
usually without `sudo`, so nothing in this chapter administers
a server; it drives one.

A note on where the output comes from. The SSH-client blocks
run on the author's Mac against a real remote Linux server (an
AWS EC2 Ubuntu box), captured by `transcripts/capture-ch13-mac.sh`;
`rsync`'s semantics, `ssh-keygen`, `tar`, and `du` run in this
book's Linux sandbox, where they need no network and behave
exactly as they do remotely. Because everything SSH touches is
identifying, the blocks show placeholders: the server's
hostname is `[hostname]`, any literal address `[ip]`, the
account name `[account]`, and the home path `~` with its
mount (`[mount]`) and device (`[device]`); the server alias is
`lab-server` throughout. No private key is ever shown. The one
keypair on display is a throwaway generated for the book.

![The laptop-to-server motion this chapter delivers. Your laptop is where you edit and version; a key and `~/.ssh/config` open an authenticated, named connection to a remote Linux box; `rsync` moves the project across (lockfiles, not built environments) and results back; `uv sync` and `renv::restore()` rebuild the environment on arrival. The same pattern reaches a GPU pod or an HPC login node, which you learn to recognize rather than master.](../assets/figures/ch13/fig-ch13-motion){#fig-ch13-motion fig-alt="A left-to-right diagram. On the left a card labeled 'laptop (edit, version, Git)'. An arrow labeled 'ssh: key + ~/.ssh/config' leads to a center card 'remote Linux box'. Between them two arrows: 'rsync -a (lockfiles travel)' pointing right and 'rsync results back' pointing left. Above the center card, 'uv sync / renv::restore() rebuild the environment'. On the right two faded cards, 'GPU pod (+ a meter)' and 'HPC login node (sbatch)', labeled 'recognize and route'." width="5.6in" fig-pos="H"}

## Reaching a shell on a machine you cannot see

`ssh` logs you into a remote host and runs commands there. Give
it an address and it opens an interactive shell; give it a
command and it runs just that one and returns, which is how
scripts and capture tools talk to a server without a human at
the keyboard:

```bash
$ ssh lab-server 'uname -a'
Linux [hostname] 6.8.0-1039-aws #41~22.04.1-Ubuntu SMP Thu Sep 11 10:54:48 UTC 2025 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

The prompt never changed, but that `uname` ran on another
machine: a different kernel, `x86_64`, and the `aws` in the
build string gives it away as a cloud VM. Most of what you
learned in Parts I and II applies directly, because on the far
side of `ssh` is an ordinary Linux shell. It is a GNU one,
though, so the BSD-versus-GNU differences this book keeps
flagging now sit between your Mac and the server rather than
inside one machine. The other new fact is that a dropped
network connection, not a closed terminal, is now what can
interrupt your work, a thread this
chapter returns to.

Be precise about what `ssh` gives you. It authenticates the two
ends to each other and encrypts everything on the wire, so you
know which machine you reached and no one between can read the
traffic. It does not, by itself, make the server "secure" or
your data safe there; those depend on how the box is
administered and on the habits from Chapter 10. The first time
you connect, `ssh` records the server's host key in
`~/.ssh/known_hosts` and adds new hosts automatically; if that
key ever changes, it "warns about this and disables password
authentication to prevent server spoofing or man-in-the-middle
attacks," per the `ssh` manual.

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
One day `ssh` refuses to connect with a wall of hashes and
`REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED!`. That warning means
the server's host key no longer matches the one in
`known_hosts`. Two very different things cause it. The benign
case: the server was rebuilt, reinstalled, or reprovisioned
(common with cloud VMs and pods), so it has a legitimately new
key. The dangerous case: something is impersonating the server.
Do not blindly delete the line to make it go away. Confirm out
of band that the machine was rebuilt (the console, a colleague,
the provider's dashboard); only then remove the stale entry
with `ssh-keygen -R lab-server` and reconnect, checking the new
fingerprint. If you cannot explain the change, stop and treat
it as an interception until proven otherwise.
:::

## Keys, and the permissions that make them work

Typing a password on every connection does not scale and cannot
be scripted. Key authentication replaces it with a pair of
files: a private key that stays on your laptop and is never
shared, and a public key you copy to each server. `ssh-keygen`
makes the pair (here a throwaway demo key, generated in scratch
for the book, never a real one):

```bash
$ ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C book-demo-key -f demo_key -N ''
Generating public/private ed25519 key pair.
Your identification has been saved in demo_key
Your public key has been saved in demo_key.pub
The key fingerprint is:
SHA256:5T0CAAnWjMoASfFhZ1nJBFsqob7lGpBqIA9jTYYfrwk book-demo-key
```

`-t ed25519` picks the modern key type (also the default),
`-C` sets a comment to recognize it by, and `-f` names the
output. Two files result: `demo_key` (private, secret) and
`demo_key.pub` (public, shareable). The public half is a single
line you can hand out freely, and the fingerprint identifies
the key without revealing it:

```bash
$ cat demo_key.pub
ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIEaqncWvaHmNESKgkhbk0F7qjjgMbz171gdQCQOYOUJz book-demo-key

# The fingerprint names the key without revealing it:
$ ssh-keygen -lf demo_key.pub
256 SHA256:5T0CAAnWjMoASfFhZ1nJBFsqob7lGpBqIA9jTYYfrwk book-demo-key (ED25519)
```

The public key goes into `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on the
server (one key per line); anyone holding the matching private
key can then log in as you. That is exactly why the private key
must stay private, and OpenSSH enforces this through file
permissions. It refuses to use a private key that other users
can read. Lock it down with Chapter 10's `chmod`:

```bash
$ chmod 600 demo_key
$ ls -l
total 8
-rw------- 1 [account] [account] 399 Jul  5 10:18 demo_key
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] [account]  95 Jul  5 10:18 demo_key.pub
```

`600` leaves the private key readable and writable only by you;
the public key can stay `644`. The directory itself should be
`700`. A private key is usually protected further by a
*passphrase* (pass `-N ''` here only to keep the demo
non-interactive), and the `ssh-agent` holds the decrypted key
in memory so you type that passphrase once per session rather
than once per connection:

```bash
$ ssh-add demo_key
Identity added: demo_key (book-demo-key)
$ ssh-add -l
256 SHA256:5T0CAAnWjMoASfFhZ1nJBFsqob7lGpBqIA9jTYYfrwk book-demo-key (ED25519)
```

A real `~/.ssh` collects these files, on both the laptop and
the server. The map:

```text
~/.ssh/                 # dir must be 700 (drwx------)
|-- config              # per-host shortcuts (Section 3)
|-- id_ed25519          # PRIVATE key, 600, never leaves
|-- id_ed25519.pub      # public key, 644, copy to servers
|-- known_hosts         # host keys ssh has seen
`-- authorized_keys     # (on the SERVER) public keys allowed in
```

The server side shows the same shape, and the `700` directory
and the `authorized_keys` file are both real:

```bash
$ ssh lab-server 'ls -ld ~/.ssh; ls -l ~/.ssh'
drwx------ 3 [account] [account] 4096 Apr  6 22:19 [mount]/[account]/.ssh
total 44
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] [account]  686 Mar 20 17:25 authorized_keys
```

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
If key authentication silently falls back to asking for a
password, the cause is almost always permissions, not the key.
OpenSSH ignores a private key that is group- or world-readable,
and `sshd` ignores an `authorized_keys` file (or a `~/.ssh`
directory, or the home directory) that others can write to. The
checklist: private key `600`, `~/.ssh` `700`, `authorized_keys`
`600` or `644`, and the home directory not group-writable, on
both machines. Chapter 10's `chmod` fixes all of them; the key
material is rarely the problem.
:::

## A config file, and forwarding a port

Retyping `ssh -p 22 -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 account@host`
every time invites mistakes. `~/.ssh/config` gives the whole
connection a name. This is an authored template, not a capture;
fill in your own values:

```text
# ~/.ssh/config
Host lab-server
    HostName [ip]                # a real IP or a DNS name
    User     your-account
    Port     22
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
```

After this, `ssh lab-server` expands to the full command, and
so does every tool that reads the same config: `rsync`, `scp`,
`git`, and your editor's remote mode. The `ssh` manual notes
that "the first obtained value for each parameter is used," so
put specific `Host` blocks above general defaults. To see what
an alias actually resolves to, ask `ssh -G lab-server`; on the
author's machine it reports `hostname [ip]`, `user [account]`,
`port 22`, and `identityfile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519`, the
resolved settings the block above produced.

Sometimes the thing you want is a service bound to the server's
*own* `localhost`, a Jupyter notebook, a database, a dashboard,
not exposed to the network at all. `ssh -L` forwards a local
port to it, so your laptop can reach it as if it were local:

```bash
$ ssh -fN -L 8899:localhost:39797 lab-server
# reach the server-side http.server THROUGH the tunnel:
$ curl -s http://localhost:8899/ | sed -n '1,12p'
<!DOCTYPE HTML PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.01//EN" "http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/strict.dtd">
<html>
<head>
<meta http-equiv="Content-Type" content="text/html; charset=utf-8">
<title>Directory listing for /</title>
</head>
<body>
<h1>Directory listing for /</h1>
<hr>
<ul>
<li><a href="HELLO.txt">HELLO.txt</a></li>
<li><a href="step.py">step.py</a></li>
```

`-L 8899:localhost:39797` reads as "forward my local `8899` to
`localhost:39797` on the server"; `-N` runs no remote command
and `-f` backgrounds the tunnel. The `curl` on the laptop
reached a server-side web server (a throwaway one here) purely
through the tunnel. The everyday version of this is running a
notebook on the server and opening it in your laptop's browser.
A related flag, `-J`, hops through a bastion or jump host when
the server is not directly reachable.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Agent forwarding (`ForwardAgent yes`, or `ssh -A`) lets a
process on the remote host use the keys in your laptop's agent,
which is convenient for, say, `git` operations from the server.
It also has a real cost. The `ssh_config` manual is blunt: a
user "with the ability to bypass file permissions on the remote
host" can reach your forwarded agent, and while they "cannot
obtain key material from the agent," they "can perform
operations on the keys that enable them to authenticate using
the identities loaded into the agent." In plain terms: on a box
where someone else is root, agent forwarding lets them
authenticate as you, to anywhere your keys reach, for as long
as you are connected. Do not forward your agent to a machine
you do not fully trust; a jump host (`-J`) or a dedicated
per-host key is usually the safer tool.
:::

## Moving the project with `rsync`

`scp file lab-server:path` copies a single file over the same
SSH connection, which is fine for one file. For a directory
tree, and for re-syncing it after edits, `rsync` is the right
tool: it transfers only what changed. Its one genuine trap is
the trailing slash on the source. A source with a trailing
slash means "the contents of this directory"; without it, "the
directory itself":

```bash
# Trailing slash on the source copies its CONTENTS into the target:
$ rsync -a proj/ backup
$ find backup | sort
backup
backup/data
backup/data/panel.csv
backup/scripts
backup/scripts/00_make.py
backup/scripts/01_clean.py

# No trailing slash nests the source dir INSIDE the target:
$ rsync -a proj nested
$ find nested | sort
nested
nested/proj
nested/proj/data
nested/proj/data/panel.csv
nested/proj/scripts
nested/proj/scripts/00_make.py
nested/proj/scripts/01_clean.py
```

`proj/` put the contents directly into `backup`; `proj` (no
slash) nested a `proj/` inside `nested`. The `rsync` manual
puts it exactly: a trailing slash "means 'copy the contents of
this directory' as opposed to 'copy the directory by name'."
`-a` (archive) preserves permissions, times, and symlinks and
recurses.

To push the running example to the server, exclude the two
directories Chapter 13 said never travel: the built
environments. The lockfiles go; `.venv/` and `renv/library/`
stay home and are rebuilt on the far side:

```bash
$ rsync -av --exclude='.venv' --exclude='renv/library' \
    sandbox/asset-pricing/ lab-server:ch13-demo/
[...]
sent 4994461 bytes  received 1024 bytes  2474114 bytes/sec
total size is 4989006  speedup is 1.00
```

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
`rsync --delete` makes the destination match the source by
*deleting* anything on the destination that is not in the
source. Aim it at the wrong directory, or fumble that trailing
slash, and it erases files `rsync` never created. There is no
trash and no undo. Always dry-run first with `-n`, and read the
`deleting` lines before committing:

```bash
$ rsync -a -v -n --delete proj/ backup
sending incremental file list
deleting OLD_RESULT.csv
./

sent 229 bytes  received 46 bytes  550.00 bytes/sec
total size is 28  speedup is 0.10 (DRY RUN)
$ ls backup
OLD_RESULT.csv
data
scripts
```

The `(DRY RUN)` marker and the intact `OLD_RESULT.csv` confirm
nothing happened yet. Only when the dry run shows exactly what
you expect do you drop the `-n`. The same file discipline as
Chapter 7's `rm -rf`: look before you leap.
:::

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
The `rsync` on macOS is not the `rsync` on Linux. Recent macOS
ships `openrsync`, an independent OpenBSD reimplementation:

```bash
$ rsync --version | head -2
openrsync: protocol version 29
rsync version 2.6.9 compatible
```

Linux ships GNU rsync, a different program at a newer protocol:

```bash
$ rsync --version | head -1
rsync  version 3.2.7  protocol version 31
```

They speak a common protocol, and the everyday flags used above
(`-a`, `-v`, `-n`, `--delete`, `--exclude`) behave the same on
both, so the transfers in this chapter are portable. But they
are different programs, and the man page you trust for one may
not describe the other. On the tested macOS, GNU-only niceties
like `--itemize-changes` and `-z` (compress) happen to be
accepted, yet an older macOS or a bare OpenBSD box may not have
them. The portable habit: stick to the common flags, and run
`rsync --version` on any new machine before assuming a flag
exists.
:::

## Did the bytes survive the trip?

Moving data is not the same as moving it *intact*, and on
restricted or licensed data an undetected truncation is a real
hazard. Chapter 11 built a manifest of `sha256sum` hashes;
across two machines, the check is the same idea, run on both
ends. After the push, hash the moved files on the server and on
the laptop and compare:

```bash
$ ssh lab-server 'cd ch13-demo && find . -name "*.csv" | sort \
    | xargs sha256sum'
54989b4637304f96016a4877d20be4b144165c2db6ee4e7720350cf4ce9b99b1  ./data/clean/portfolio.csv
8648c3be59440b8be87b9a473a10a029b3e50f33a7af7bd454445de6fd31c7af  ./data/raw/factors.csv
5056f91931a3c99e95aa79125c22fc4bc025c49210c99ff52b9dcef6352a92af  ./data/raw/firm_panel.csv

# ...and on the MAC with shasum -a 256 (Ch 3's divergence):
$ find . -name '*.csv' | sort | xargs shasum -a 256
54989b4637304f96016a4877d20be4b144165c2db6ee4e7720350cf4ce9b99b1  ./data/clean/portfolio.csv
8648c3be59440b8be87b9a473a10a029b3e50f33a7af7bd454445de6fd31c7af  ./data/raw/factors.csv
5056f91931a3c99e95aa79125c22fc4bc025c49210c99ff52b9dcef6352a92af  ./data/raw/firm_panel.csv
```

Identical hashes, from GNU `sha256sum` on the server and BSD
`shasum -a 256` on the Mac (Chapter 3's divergence, earning its
keep). The bytes that arrived are the bytes that left.

Be careful what you credit `rsync` with here. The `rsync`
manual says it "always verifies that each transferred file was
correctly reconstructed on the receiving side by checking a
whole-file checksum," so a garbled transfer is caught in
flight. But that is a transfer-integrity check, not proof
against an already-corrupt source, and it "has nothing to do
with" a checksum you run end to end against a known-good
manifest. The two are complementary: `rsync` guards the wire,
your `sha256sum` guards the file.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Two independent guarantees make a remote run trustworthy, and
they combine. A checksum-verified transfer means the *same
bytes* sit on both machines: the raw data is identical, not
merely "copied." Rebuilding the environment from the lockfiles
(next section) means the *same package versions* run on both
machines. Same inputs, same code versions, on the laptop and on
the server. Neither Chapter 11's manifest nor Chapter 13's
lockfile is re-credited for the other's job; together they are
what let a result computed on a server be believed back on the
laptop.
:::

Two housekeeping tools travel with this. `tar` bundles a tree
into one file (optionally gzip-compressed) so it moves as a
single object and unpacks identically:

```bash
$ tar -czf proj.tgz proj
$ ls -lh proj.tgz
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] [account] 282 Jul  4 13:43 proj.tgz
$ tar -tzf proj.tgz
proj/
proj/data/
proj/data/panel.csv
proj/scripts/
proj/scripts/00_make.py
```

And before you move anything, `du` shows what is worth moving,
while `df` (run on the server) shows whether there is room:

```bash
$ du -h --max-depth=1 proj | sort -h
56K	proj/output
300K	proj/data
360K	proj
```

```bash
$ ssh lab-server 'df -h $HOME'
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
[device]    492G  405G   63G  87% [mount]
```

`ncdu` is the interactive, navigable version of `du`; because
it is a full-screen TUI rather than a one-shot command, it
belongs with the modern-kit tools in Chapter 16.

## Rebuilding the environment, and surviving a disconnect (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

The project is now on the server without its environments, and
this is the moment Chapter 13 was built for. `uv sync` reads
`uv.lock` and rebuilds the Python environment, resolving the
universal lock for whatever interpreter the server has:

```bash
$ ssh lab-server 'cd ch13-demo && ~/.local/bin/uv sync'
Using CPython 3.12.12
Creating virtual environment at: .venv
Resolved 28 packages in 45ms
[...]
Installed 18 packages in 217ms
```

Eighteen packages here, resolved for the server's CPython 3.12;
the same lock gave 20 on the sandbox's CPython 3.10, because a
universal lockfile resolves per interpreter (Chapter 13's
divergence, live on a third machine). `statsmodels` lands at
`0.14.6`, the locked version. Note the explicit
`~/.local/bin/uv`: a non-interactive `ssh host 'cmd'` does not
read your login shell's startup files, so a tool installed in
`~/.local/bin` is not on `PATH` unless you name it in full, a
seam Chapter 18 returns to.

R rebuilds the same way, though more slowly. This server had no
`renv` and no copy of the library, so `renv::restore()`, run
over `ssh` in the demo directory, first bootstraps `renv`, then
rebuilds the library from `renv.lock` by compiling every
package from source:

```bash
- Downloading renv ... OK
- Installing renv  ... OK
[...]
- lattice       [0.20-45 -> 0.22-9]
- nlme          [3.1-160 -> 3.1-168]
- base64enc     [* -> 0.1-6]
- bslib         [* -> 0.11.0]
[...]
Successfully installed 35 packages in 420 seconds.
```

Seven minutes of compilation, and the library matches the lock:
`fixest 0.14.2`, the same version Chapter 8 read and Chapter 13
committed. Two details are worth seeing. `lattice` and `nlme`
show `[old -> new]` because the server already had system
copies that `renv` overrides to the locked versions; the rest
show `[* -> version]` because they were absent. Either way the
installed versions are the lock's, not whatever the server
happened to have: a lockfile pins *packages*, not the R
interpreter itself, exactly the honest edge Chapter 13 drew
(renv tracks the R version but does not install it, so a
server on a different R patch release restores the same
packages, not the same R).

One new hazard comes with remoteness. On your laptop, a job
ends when you decide; over `ssh`, the connection is a lifeline.
Close the laptop or lose the network and the remote shell
receives `SIGHUP`, and by default the job dies with it. Chapter
10's `nohup` is the fire-and-forget stopgap: the job survives
the disconnect, but you cannot reattach to it and drive it, only
follow its log from the outside. The real answer, a session you
can detach from, walk
away, and reattach to later to watch a job run live, is `tmux`,
which the next chapter is about.

## The bigger remote worlds: GPU pods and HPC (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

Two larger remote settings are worth recognizing, because the
skills above carry straight into them and the goal is to route
yourself, not to master either here.

A cloud GPU pod (Runpod and its kind) is, from the shell's
point of view, just another Linux box you `ssh` into, plus a
meter running. The same `ssh`, `rsync`, and `uv sync` all
apply. What differs is three things worth internalizing before
you rent one: there is a GPU (check it with `nvidia-smi`;
`nvtop` is the live monitor), a pod's container storage is
often *ephemeral*, so you `rsync` results *off* before you stop
the pod (or write them to a persistent volume, if the provider
offers one), and billing is metered, on Runpod by the second,
so an idle pod still costs money. This book runs nothing on a
paid pod; the point is only that it is not a new kind of
machine, just a metered one.

A university HPC cluster adds a scheduler, and here the change
is real: you do not run heavy jobs on the login node you land
on. You write a small job script and *submit* it to a scheduler
(usually Slurm), which queues it and runs it on a compute node
when resources free up. Three verbs are enough to recognize the
world: `sbatch` submits a job script (whose `#SBATCH` comment
lines set resources), `squeue` shows the queue, and `scancel`
cancels a job. A minimal script, shown to be read, not run
here:

```text
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#SBATCH --job-name=ff5
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4
#SBATCH --mem=8G
#SBATCH --time=00:30:00

module load R/4.5
Rscript scripts/04_regression.R
```

Clusters also use *modules* (`module load ...`) instead of a
system-wide install, plus *scratch* space and per-user
*quotas*. This is a signpost, not a manual: when you land on a
real cluster, its own documentation is the authority. The same
goes for editor integrations like VS Code's Remote-SSH, which
opens a folder on the server and edits it as if local over the
very same `ssh` config; it is a convenience worth naming and
reading the official docs for, not a new mechanism.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Use the running example and your own remote box (or borrow the
patterns if you have none yet).

1. Name the connection. Add a `Host` block for your server to
   `~/.ssh/config`, then confirm `ssh myalias 'hostname'`
   returns the server's name and `ssh -G myalias` shows the
   `hostname`, `user`, and `identityfile` you expect.

2. Move the project, safely. From the project root, dry-run the
   push first: `rsync -av -n --exclude='.venv'
   --exclude='renv/library' ./ myalias:ch13-demo/`. Read the
   list, then run it for real without `-n`. State in one
   sentence why `.venv/` and `renv/library/` are excluded.

3. Prove the trailing slash to yourself. In a throwaway `/tmp`
   directory, run `rsync -a src/ a` and `rsync -a src b`, then
   `find a` and `find b`, and explain the difference in one
   line. Do NOT test `--delete` anywhere real; if you must,
   only in `/tmp` with `-n` first.

4. Verify the bytes. `sha256sum` (or `shasum -a 256`) a data
   file on both ends and confirm the hashes match. Then rebuild
   the Python environment on the server with `uv sync` and
   check that the installed `statsmodels` version matches the
   one in `uv.lock`.
